content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Dart is one of the best languages most developers have never chosen on purpose. Google built it, saved it, and has carried it for fifteen years. Its next leap won’t come from Flutter. It’ll come from a bigger ecosystem, when people reach for Dart on its own, and when more than one company helps it grow.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A while back I was catching up with a senior developer I respect, and I mentioned I’d been picking up Dart for cross-platform work. He barely let me finish, and said “What is Dart? Just learn Flutter.”
I’ve heard some version of that more times than I’d like. Even the AI tools I use assume the two are the same thing. I eventually had to set a standing instruction, in all caps, that Dart is not Flutter. But that reflex isn’t really the point. It’s a symptom of something bigger: almost everyone meets Dart through Flutter, so it’s easy never to think of building anything else with it.
I’m not a language designer or an insider, just someone who uses Dart and keeps an eye on where it’s going. But you don’t have to be an expert to notice when something good keeps getting passed over. I think Dart’s real potential is still ahead of it, and it gets unlocked the moment people start reaching for Dart outside Flutter. For that to happen, the whole ecosystem has to grow, and that’s not something one company can do by itself.
None of this is a knock on Flutter or the teams at Google who built all of this. I’m genuinely a fan. This is a hope for Dart, not a complaint about anyone.
A language worth being grateful for
Dart is really good. It compiles ahead of time to native code across platforms, to JavaScript, and to WebAssembly via WasmGC, and its type system is sound and modern in ways the industry has spent years catching up to, and that’s before the long list of smaller, lovable touches you notice once you’re actually writing it. None of that was luck: Dart exists because Google built it and then kept paying for it, year after year. That deserves credit. When the Dart team set out in 2011 to improve on JavaScript, they were early, not wrong. JavaScript had piled up years of inconsistencies and quiet traps, and they read the problem almost exactly right.
But being good was never really Dart’s problem. The harder question has always been who it belongs to, and to see why that matters, it helps to go back to the beginning.
What the early years taught us
Dart was supposed to win a very different way. The original plan was bold: ship the Dart virtual machine inside Chrome, so Dart could run natively in Google’s own browser while everyone else got compiled-to-JavaScript output. For the team that also happened to build the browser, that made a certain kind of sense.
But web history has a hard lesson here. In the late 1990s, Microsoft shipped VBScript as a browser language, and it only ran in Internet Explorer — no rival had any reason to adopt a competitor’s language, so it never became a shared standard and eventually faded out. People spotted the same risk for Dart right away; as early as 2011, SitePoint’s Craig Buckler predicted it would “fail in the same way VBScript did in Internet Explorer.”
That’s roughly how it played out. Other browsers had no real reason to adopt a language tied so tightly to one company, and Google, to its credit, read the room and dropped the plan in 2015. The bigger version of Dart didn’t stall because the engineering was bad. It stalled because that particular vision needed rival browsers to adopt a competitor’s language as a shared standard, something no company was ever going to do for another, no matter how good the language or the intentions. Dart itself launched just fine; it was the cross-vendor web standard that couldn’t.
The comeback, and what it owes to Google
Dart’s second life came through Flutter, and that comeback is a real success story. A lot of the credit goes to Google, and plenty to the community that rallied around it. Flutter didn’t get popular by accident. Google poured money into the engineering and the docs, sent people out to developer conferences, and kept up a marketing push that eventually put Flutter in front of millions of developers. Today serious companies ship real products on it, and Dart is, by any fair measure, back. That’s a direct payoff on Google’s long bet, and it’s worth celebrating.
There’s a bittersweet part to this, though. Because most developers meet Dart through Flutter, what it can do outside Flutter mostly goes unexplored. That’s how it started for me: someone recommended Flutter, and Dart was simply what came with it. Then I started writing Dart on its own, and I fell for the language itself. It’s a strange fate for something this good: everywhere as Flutter’s engine, and rarely reached for on its own.
Here’s something worth appreciating, too: a lot of what makes Flutter feel so good comes from Dart underneath. Its beloved hot reload is really the Dart VM swapping in your changes as you type, and Dart is also what compiles one codebase to native, JavaScript, and WebAssembly.
Why a foundation is the natural next step
This is where a foundation comes in. Not to take anything from Google, but to give Dart room that Google alone can’t. The risk of leaning on a single owner isn’t hypothetical: when Google cut staff from its Flutter and Dart teams in 2024, community confidence wobbled almost overnight, not because the work stopped, but because everything still rode on one company’s shifting priorities. A neutral, non-profit foundation looks after a language on behalf of a whole industry instead of a single owner, and that shift changes how everyone else treats it.
And this isn’t a hypothetical fix. It’s worked before, more than once. When Mozilla helped set up the Rust Foundation in 2021, the founding members were AWS, Google, Microsoft, Huawei, and Mozilla itself. Those companies compete hard, and they still funded a shared language together, precisely because none of them owned it. The .NET Foundation helped turn .NET from a Microsoft project into something the rest of the world could treat as genuinely open. The Linux Foundation, the OpenJS Foundation, the CNCF: it’s the same idea every time. Shared, neutral governance is what lets even rivals invest in the same infrastructure.
And that kind of investment usually isn’t charity. It’s good business. Big tech makes far more on top of open-source projects like Linux and Kubernetes than it ever spends keeping them healthy. If you profit from something, helping fund it is just smart. A Dart Foundation would be the same deal: a capable, widely usable language, paid for by the companies that gain from building on it.
A foundation could give Dart that same room. Dart is already open source, with a public roadmap and spec. What’s missing is a say in setting them for anyone outside Google, along with maintainers and funding from other companies. Most important, it could pull Dart into the corners of software where developers don’t yet think to use it, growing an ecosystem well beyond Flutter. Google wouldn’t have to walk away; it could stay the biggest contributor while finally giving everyone else a seat at the table.
And this isn’t just wishful thinking anymore. Google has started weighing the idea itself: a Google-run survey recently asked developers how their trust would change if Flutter were handed to an independent, non-profit foundation like the Linux Foundation or Apache. Tellingly, the question was only about Flutter, but freeing Flutter barely matters if the language underneath it stays owned by one company. You can’t really hand Flutter to the industry while Dart, the thing it runs on, is still Google’s alone. If anything, that makes the case for Dart: the foundation conversation is already happening, and Dart shouldn’t be left out of it as mere Flutter baggage.
I don’t think a foundation is a smaller dream than the one Google started with. I think it’s the same dream, finally pointed the right way. Back in 2011 the goal was a language that belonged to the whole web, and the thing that stalled it is exactly what a neutral foundation now fixes: it gives rivals a stake instead of asking them to back a competitor’s project. So this isn’t Dart settling down. It’s the original vision getting the vehicle it always needed.
The honest objections
Any honest case has to admit where it’s weak.
The first is funding. Google pays for most of Dart’s world today, and a foundation only helps if other companies actually show up alongside it. A foundation that’s Google-funded in all but name would just change the letterhead. The point is shared investment, not a new logo.
The second one is harder. A lot of Dart’s momentum still runs through Flutter, not the language by itself. If it turns out there just isn’t much demand for Dart outside Flutter, then neutral governance won’t move the needle much. A foundation can remove a barrier to adoption. It can’t invent interest that was never there to begin with.
The third is timing. Foundations tend to work best when they’re set up from a position of strength, the way Rust’s was, and not as a last-ditch rescue. The good news is that’s exactly where Dart is right now. It’s healthy, it’s widely used, and its own creator is willing to ask the question out loud. That makes this a good moment to do it, not a desperate one.
The case, plainly
None of this is me saying Dart needs to be a better language. It’s already good. My point is different, and I think harder to argue with. A language this capable deserves to be known on its own terms and backed by more than one company. The way you get there is an independent, non-profit foundation.
Google gave Dart its start, and then its second wind. The most exciting next chapter isn’t Google carrying this alone. It’s Google sharing what it built, and letting Dart step out from behind Flutter, carry its own name, and become something the whole industry can invest in. That wouldn’t take anything away from what Google made. If anything, it might be the best proof of how good it was all along.
Over to you
I wrote this to start a conversation, not to end one, so I’d genuinely like to hear where you land:

• Do you think of Dart as a language in its own right, or mostly as “the thing Flutter uses”?

• Would an independent foundation change how much you’d trust Dart?

• Do you use Dart outside Flutter at all?

• Would you reach for Dart more often if the major cloud providers backed it directly?
At the bottom of it, I just love Dart. I’ve come to trust it, and I really do believe its ecosystem takes off the day it stands on its own under a foundation; a future I’d like to help bring a little closer.
If you’d like to help too, drop a comment — questions and disagreements especially welcome — and like or share this article if it resonated. Every bit nudges the conversation forward. I’ll be reading and replying.
